title: Ovulation Calculator: Track Fertile Window & Cycle Days 2026
h1: Ovulation and Menstrual Cycle Calendar Calculator
description: Calculate your ovulation day and 6-day fertile window in seconds. Enter your cycle length to predict your most fertile days and plan pregnancy naturally.
intro: Ovulation typically happens **14 days before the next period**, regardless of cycle length. If your cycle is 28 days, ovulation is around day 14; if it's 32 days, ovulation is around day 18. The **fertile window** covers 5 days before ovulation plus the day itself (sperm survives up to 5 days in fertile cervical mucus; the egg survives 12–24 hours). This calculator estimates your ovulation day, fertile window and predicted next period based on your last menstrual date and average cycle length. Useful for natural family planning, conception planning, tracking fertility symptoms and confirming diagnoses of irregular cycles. For maximum accuracy combine with basal body temperature, LH strips or cervical mucus observation.
keyTakeaway: The **fertile window** lasts about **6 days**: sperm survive up to 5 days, and the egg lives 12–24 hours. The most fertile day is the **day before ovulation**.
useCases: You're trying to conceive and want to know your most fertile days.
You use the calendar method as a complement to contraception.
You want to plan intercourse to maximize the chances of conception.
Your OB/GYN asked you to record your cycle and ovulation dates.
You want to better understand your menstrual cycle and its phases.

fields.label: First day of your last period
Cycle length (days)
fields.help: Count from the first day of one period to the first day of the next

example.title: Example: LMP April 1, 28-day cycle
steps: **Fertile window**: **April 10 to April 15** (5 days before + day of ovulation).
example.result: Your ovulation is approximately **April 15**. Your fertile window runs from **April 10 to April 15**. Next period: **April 29**.

explanation:
## ¿Qué es la ovulación?

La ovulación es la liberación del óvulo desde el ovario, típicamente 14 días antes del próximo período. La ventana fértil dura 6 días: 5 previos a la ovulación (cuando los espermatozoides sobreviven) más el día de ovulación mismo. El día más fértil es el anterior a la ovulación, cuando la probabilidad de embarazo es máxima.

## How it's calculated

The algorithm uses the **Ogino method** (1930) validated by modern fertility research:

```
ovulation_day = last_period_date + (cycle_length − 14)
fertile_window = [ovulation_day − 5, ovulation_day + 1]
next_period = last_period_date + cycle_length
```

The 14-day luteal phase is remarkably stable across women (range 11–16 days), while the follicular phase varies with cycle length.

## Reference table 2026

| Cycle length | Ovulation day | Fertile window |
|---|---|---|
| 24 days | Day 10 | Days 5–11 |
| 28 days | Day 14 | Days 9–15 |
| 30 days | Day 16 | Days 11–17 |
| 32 days | Day 18 | Days 13–19 |
| 35 days | Day 21 | Days 16–22 |

Data based on ACOG guidelines and Wilcox et al. (NEJM 1995) on timing of intercourse and conception probability.

## Special cases

- **Irregular cycles (PCOS, thyroid)**: calendar method is unreliable; use LH strips or ultrasound.
- **Breastfeeding**: ovulation can return before first period; calendar method not valid.
- **Postpartum**: first 3 cycles after birth tend to be anovulatory.
- **Perimenopause**: luteal phase shortens; fertility drops but pregnancy still possible.
- **Hormonal contraception withdrawal**: cycles may be irregular for 1–3 months; use symptom tracking.

## Common mistakes

1. **Assuming day 14 for every woman**: this only applies to a 28-day cycle; longer cycles ovulate later.
2. **Ignoring the 5-day sperm lifespan**: fertile window starts before ovulation, not on ovulation day.
3. **Using calendar method as contraception**: failure rate 10–25% per year; not reliable without backup.
4. **Not tracking multiple cycles**: use at least 6 months of data for reliable prediction.
5. **Confusing spotting with period start**: count day 1 as first day of full flow.

## Sources

Evidence from ACOG and peer-reviewed reproductive research.

faq.q: How long does the fertile window last?
faq.a: The fertile window lasts approximately **6 days**: the 5 days before ovulation + the day of ovulation. The day with the highest chance of conception is the **day before ovulation** (~30% probability).

faq.q: How do I know how long my menstrual cycle is?
faq.a: Count from the **first day of bleeding** of one period to the **first day of bleeding** of the next. Do this for 3–6 months and take the average. A normal cycle lasts between **21 and 35 days**.

faq.q: Can I get pregnant outside the fertile window?
faq.a: It's **very unlikely** but not impossible, especially if your cycles are irregular and ovulation happens at a different time than estimated. Sperm can survive up to 5 days, so an inaccurate calculation could miss fertile days.

The calendar method (Ogino-Knaus) has **75–88% effectiveness** with typical use, well below modern methods (pill 91%, IUD 99%). **It is not recommended as a primary contraceptive method**. Consult your OB/GYN.

faq.q: When is the best time to have sex if I want to conceive?
faq.a: The **2–3 days before ovulation** are the most fertile. Ideally, have sex **every 1–2 days throughout the fertile window** (5 days before ovulation through the day of ovulation).

faq.q: Does the calculator work with irregular cycles?
faq.a: With irregular cycles the estimate is less precise. If your cycle varies by more than 5 days between one and the next, combine the calendar method with basal temperature and LH tests.

faq.q: What is the luteal phase and how long does it last?
faq.a: The **luteal phase** is the period between ovulation and the next period. It normally lasts **12–16 days** (average 14). It's fairly constant in each woman. A luteal phase shorter than 10 days can indicate a hormonal issue that makes implantation difficult.

sources.title: ACOG — Fertility Awareness-Based Methods
sources.publisher: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists

sources.title: Wilcox et al. — Timing of Intercourse and Probability of Conception

howToSteps.name: Enter your LMP
howToSteps.text: Select the first day of your last menstrual period.

howToSteps.name: Enter your cycle length
howToSteps.text: Enter how many days your cycle lasts (e.g., 28). If unsure, use 28 as the average.

howToSteps.name: Click Calculate
howToSteps.text: Press Calculate to see your ovulation date and fertile window.

howToSteps.name: Review the results
howToSteps.text: You'll see the ovulation date, fertile window, and next estimated period.
